question.question: Reviewing SOP-CLIN-001 (Clinical Decision Support System Validation) against EU AI Act Article 11 (technical documentation) and Article 17 (quality management system), what's MISSING from the SOP?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) coverage of all Annex IV documentation elements — general description, design specifications, system architecture, training/testing/validation data and methodology, post-market monitoring plan, declaration of conformity, instructions for use; (2) explicit linkage between the QMS components and Article 17 sub-elements — regulatory compliance strategy, design control, data management, technical documentation, post-market monitoring system (Article 72), incident reporting (Article 73), communication with authorities, record-keeping, resource management; (3) traceability matrix mapping each Annex IV / Article 17 element to specific SOPs or evidence artifacts; (4) responsible roles for each QMS element.

output.answer:
## Bottom line

SOP-CLIN-001 is a strong **clinical validation SOP**, but it is **not sufficient by itself** to meet the EU AI Act requirements for:  

- **Article 11 — Technical documentation**, which must be prepared before placing a high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service, kept up to date, and contain at least the information in **Annex IV**.  
- **Article 17 — Quality management system**, which requires a documented, systematic QMS covering regulatory compliance, design/development, testing/validation, data management, risk management, post-market monitoring, incident reporting, communications, recordkeeping, resources, and accountability.

The SOP contains useful validation, lockbox, statistical analysis, monitoring, and escalation controls, but several required EU AI Act elements are either only referenced at a high level or missing procedurally.

---

## What is missing for EU AI Act Article 11 / Annex IV technical documentation

| Missing item | Why it matters under EU AI Act | SOP gap |
|---|---|---|
| **A formal Annex IV technical documentation dossier structure and ownership** | Article 11 requires technical documentation to be drawn up before market placement or use, kept up to date, and sufficiently clear and comprehensive for authorities/notified bodies to assess compliance; Annex IV defines minimum contents. | SOP §5.5 says the Final Validation Report “includes all Annex IV documentation elements,” but it does not define a required Annex IV dossier, document owner, required sections, approval workflow, update triggers, or retention location. |
| **General system description required by Annex IV** | Annex IV requires a general description of the AI system, including intended purpose, provider identity, system version, interaction with hardware/software, user interface, instructions for use, and deployment forms. | SOP has scope and examples in §1, but lacks a mandatory template requiring intended purpose, provider/manufacturer details, system versioning, user interface description, hardware/software dependencies, IFU linkage, and deployment configuration. |
| **Detailed architecture, algorithm logic, and design specifications** | Annex IV requires detailed description of system elements and development process, including design specifications, logic of the AI system, algorithms, key design choices, assumptions, and architecture. | SOP focuses on validation execution, code freeze, containers, and performance analysis (§5.4–§5.5). It does not require documentation of model architecture, algorithm logic, design rationale, assumptions, model limitations, or component interactions. |
| **Third-party and pre-trained component documentation** | Annex IV requires description of system components, including where relevant third-party components and pre-trained models used in development. | SOP scope includes third-party/licensed algorithms (§1.2), and vendor controls cover CROs/readers (§6.2), but the SOP does not require third-party model documentation, provenance, licenses, performance evidence, supplier attestations, or inherited-risk documentation. |
| **Training data provenance and datasheets** | Annex IV requires documentation of data requirements, training methodologies, data provenance, data scope and characteristics, data preparation, labeling, cleaning, and other data operations. | SOP documents validation dataset acquisition, de-identification, lockbox controls, and gold-standard annotation (§5.2–§5.3), but it does not require training dataset datasheets, provenance records, labeling methodology, data representativeness analysis, data quality criteria, retention rules, or preprocessing documentation for training data. |
| **Human oversight design documentation** | Annex IV requires information on human oversight measures, and high-risk AI systems must be documented so users can interpret outputs and oversee the system. | SOP states CDSS is advisory and does not replace clinical judgment (§4), but it does not require human oversight design documentation: user roles, override mechanics, alert explanations, escalation to clinicians, training for deployers, or human-in-the-loop limitations. |
| **Robustness and cybersecurity evidence as part of technical documentation** | Annex IV requires information on system performance, limitations, robustness, cybersecurity, and foreseeable circumstances affecting operation. | SOP includes validation environment controls and container signing (§6.1), but lacks required robustness testing, adversarial testing, fail-safe behavior, resilience testing, cybersecurity test evidence, vulnerability management linkage, and foreseeable misuse analysis. |
| **Logging capability documentation** | Annex IV requires information relevant to monitoring, functioning, control, and logging capabilities of the AI system. | SOP logs validation runs and access to validation assets (§5.4, §6.1), but does not require documentation of production AI-system logs, log content, retention, auditability, user action logs, model output logs, or log review responsibilities. |
| **Lifecycle change history in the technical file** | Annex IV requires documentation of changes made to the system through its lifecycle. | SOP references annual revalidation and related change-management SOP-CLIN-005 (§7.3, §10.1), but does not require the Annex IV technical documentation to be updated after model changes, data changes, threshold changes, user-interface changes, or deployment-environment changes. |
| **Harmonised standards / common specifications compliance matrix** | Annex IV requires identification of harmonised standards or common specifications applied, or other solutions adopted to meet requirements. | SOP lists external standards in §10.2, but does not require a standard-by-standard applicability matrix, evidence mapping, deviations, or rationale where standards are not fully applied. |
| **EU Declaration of Conformity linkage** | Annex IV requires a copy of the EU declaration of conformity. | SOP does not mention creation, review, approval, storage, or linkage of the EU Declaration of Conformity. |
| **Conformity assessment evidence package** | Article 11 documentation must allow notified bodies or competent authorities to assess compliance. | SOP focuses on clinical validation approval (§5.6), but does not define a conformity-assessment-ready evidence package, notified body submission controls, or regulatory review gate before EU market placement. |

---

## What is missing for EU AI Act Article 17 quality management system

| Missing item | Why it matters under EU AI Act | SOP gap |
|---|---|---|
| **A complete AI Act QMS policy framework** | Article 17 requires providers of high-risk AI systems to establish a documented QMS with written policies, procedures, and instructions organized systematically. | SOP-CLIN-001 is a validation SOP, not a complete QMS. It references related SOPs (§10.1), but does not define the overall AI Act QMS structure, document hierarchy, process map, QMS owner, or cross-procedure evidence map. |
| **Regulatory compliance strategy, including conformity assessment** | Article 17 requires a regulatory compliance strategy, including compliance with conformity assessment procedures and management of modifications. | SOP has “Regulatory Alignment” language (§4.6) and Regulatory Affairs consultation (§3), but lacks a concrete EU AI Act regulatory strategy, conformity assessment pathway, notified-body decision tree, CE/AI Act release gate, and modification-management process. |
| **Design and development control procedures** | Article 17 requires procedures for design, design control, design verification, and development of high-risk AI systems. | SOP covers validation, but design/development controls are mostly outside scope and only referenced via SOP-DS-001 (§10.1). Missing are design input/output controls, requirements traceability, design reviews, verification criteria, design transfer, and approval gates. |
| **Verification process separate from validation** | Article 17 requires testing, validation, and verification procedures. | SOP is strong on clinical validation and statistical analysis (§5.1–§5.5), but lacks explicit software/model verification activities, requirements verification, unit/integration/system testing evidence, traceability, acceptance criteria, and independent verification review. |
| **Comprehensive data management system** | Article 17 requires systems and procedures for data management, including acquisition, collection, analysis, labeling, storage, filtration, mining, aggregation, retention, and other data operations. | SOP covers validation lockbox and de-identification (§5.2), but does not cover end-to-end data governance for training, tuning, validation, production monitoring, retention, deletion, lineage, data quality, labeling governance, or dataset change control. |
| **Integrated Article 9 risk management system** | Article 17 requires the QMS to include the risk management system referred to in Article 9. | SOP references FMEA/risk management through SOP-CLIN-002 (§10.1) and has escalation procedures (§8), but does not require risk-control traceability from hazard analysis to validation tests, residual-risk acceptance, benefit-risk review, or risk-file updates. |
| **Post-market monitoring system mapped to AI Act Article 72** | Article 17 requires inclusion of a post-market monitoring system under Article 72. | SOP has strong post-deployment monitoring elements (§7), but it does not explicitly map the PMCF/surveillance plan to AI Act Article 72, define AI Act post-market monitoring outputs, or require feeding monitoring results back into the technical documentation and QMS. |
| **Serious incident reporting procedure under AI Act Article 73** | Article 17 requires procedures for reporting serious incidents under Article 73. | SOP §8 mentions regulatory reporting after RCA and breach notification, but does not define AI Act “serious incident” criteria, reporting timelines, responsible authority, required report contents, escalation decision tree, or follow-up reporting. |
| **Communications with authorities, notified bodies, operators, customers, and interested parties** | Article 17 requires procedures for handling communications with competent authorities, notified bodies, other operators, customers, and other interested parties. | SOP identifies Regulatory Affairs as liaison (§3), but lacks a formal communication procedure, authority request handling, notified-body correspondence controls, customer/deployer safety notices, and records of regulatory communications. |
| **QMS recordkeeping procedure** | Article 17 requires systems and procedures for recordkeeping of all relevant documentation and information. | SOP stores final approval artifacts in MDMS (§5.6) and logs validation activities, but does not specify QMS-wide record retention periods, required records, archival controls, retrieval timelines, audit readiness, or evidence completeness checks. |
| **Resource management and security of supply** | Article 17 requires resource management, including security-of-supply-related measures. | SOP has roles and training (§3, §9), but lacks resource planning, competence sufficiency, infrastructure availability, cloud/vendor continuity, supply-chain resilience, service dependency management, and contingency planning. |
| **Management accountability framework beyond validation RACI** | Article 17 requires an accountability framework setting out responsibilities of management and other staff for all covered QMS aspects. | SOP has a validation RACI (§3), but not a full QMS accountability framework for regulatory compliance, design controls, data governance, incident reporting, post-market monitoring, technical documentation, and conformity assessment. |
| **CAPA / corrective action linkage** | Article 17’s QMS structure, together with AI Act obligations on corrective actions, requires systematic handling of nonconformities and corrective actions. | SOP has exception handling and RCA after incidents (§8), but lacks a formal CAPA process: nonconformity intake, root-cause classification, corrective/preventive action approval, effectiveness checks, recurrence monitoring, and QMS trend review. |
| **Supplier and third-party AI component controls** | Article 17 QMS coverage should support compliance across the provider’s development and operational processes, including third-party components where used. | SOP has vendor risk management for CROs and reader panels (§6.2), but not for third-party AI models, software libraries, cloud services, labeling vendors, data suppliers, or external model providers. |

---

## Key remediation actions

1. Add an **EU AI Act Annex IV Technical Documentation Procedure** or appendix to SOP-CLIN-001 with required dossier sections, owners, templates, update triggers, and approval workflow.  
2. Add a mandatory **Annex IV evidence matrix** mapping each technical documentation requirement to controlled records: model card, data sheets, architecture document, risk file, validation report, cybersecurity report, human oversight plan, logs, standards matrix, DoC, and PMS plan.  
3. Expand the SOP or cross-reference controlled procedures for **design controls, development controls, verification, model change control, and conformity assessment**.  
4. Add an Article 17 **QMS mapping appendix** showing where each required QMS element is implemented across Meridian SOPs.  
5. Add AI Act-specific procedures for **serious incident reporting, competent authority/notified body communications, post-market monitoring feedback loops, record retention, CAPA, supplier controls, and resource/security-of-supply management**.  
6. Require the Final Validation Report to be only one component of the technical documentation, not the entire Annex IV compliance artifact.

Sources: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, **Article 11** and **Annex IV** technical documentation requirements; Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, **Article 17** quality management system requirements. Public text available at: https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/11, https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/annex/4, and https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/17.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer thoroughly identifies missing elements for both Article 11/Annex IV and Article 17, aligning closely with the reference, and correctly cites the relevant EU AI Act articles and annex.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) against GDPR's transparency and transfer obligations, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) or alternative Chapter V transfer mechanisms for cross-border filings — international transfers are acknowledged but the operational mechanism is not specified; (2) transfer-impact-assessment procedures considering the legal environment of recipient jurisdictions; (3) Article 13/14 specificity in privacy notices triggered by filing-related processing — purposes, lawful basis, recipients (regulators), retention, data-subject rights; (4) supplementary measures (encryption, pseudonymisation) tied to high-risk filings; (5) records of processing (Article 30) specifically for filing-related processing activities.

output.answer:
SOP-LEGC-005 is **not fully aligned** to GDPR transparency and cross-border transfer obligations. It contains only a high-level GDPR statement in **SOP §4.6** that data subjects are informed via website privacy notices and that “appropriate safeguards” are used for EU-to-U.S. transfers, but it does not operationalize the required GDPR elements.

## Missing transparency elements

1. **No required privacy-notice content checklist for regulatory filings**  
   The SOP does not require filings or related privacy notices to include the full information required when personal data is collected directly from data subjects, including: controller identity/contact details, DPO contact details, purposes and legal basis, recipients/categories of recipients, retention period, data-subject rights, complaint rights, and whether provision of data is statutory/contractual. These are required under **GDPR Art. 13(1)–(2)**.  
   *SOP gap:* §4.6 merely says data subjects are informed through website privacy notices; it does not specify required content.

2. **No Article 14 process for data not obtained directly from the data subject**  
   Regulatory filings may use employee, patient, customer, model, incident, or vendor data sourced from internal systems or third parties. The SOP does not require Article 14 notices where data is not collected directly from the individual, including categories of personal data, source of the data, and timing of notice. GDPR requires this under **GDPR Art. 14(1)–(3)**.  
   *SOP gap:* No procedure identifies whether data was obtained directly or indirectly, or whether Article 14 notice is required.

3. **No standard for transparency format and accessibility**  
   GDPR requires information to be provided in a concise, transparent, intelligible, and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language under **GDPR Art. 12(1)**.  
   *SOP gap:* The SOP references privacy notices but does not require review for clarity, accessibility, language, version control, or filing-specific disclosures.

4. **No linkage between each filing and the applicable privacy notice/processing purpose**  
   GDPR transparency requires informing individuals of the purposes and legal basis for processing under **GDPR Art. 13(1)(c)** and **Art. 14(1)(c)**.  
   *SOP gap:* The Filing Calendar fields in §5.1 include statutory citation, addressee, due date, owner, materiality, and submission method, but not GDPR purpose, legal basis, data-subject population, notice reference, or privacy-notice version.

5. **No requirement to disclose recipients or categories of recipients**  
   GDPR requires disclosure of recipients or categories of recipients of personal data under **GDPR Art. 13(1)(e)** and **Art. 14(1)(e)**.  
   *SOP gap:* The SOP does not require identification of regulators, supervisory authorities, portals, vendors, counsel, analytics teams, or other recipients as GDPR-relevant recipient categories in the transparency review.

6. **No retention-period disclosure tied to filing archives**  
   GDPR requires notice of the retention period or criteria used to determine it under **GDPR Art. 13(2)(a)** and **Art. 14(2)(a)**.  
   *SOP gap:* §5.5 describes archival in iManage and WORM storage, but the SOP does not require a retention schedule, retention rationale, or corresponding disclosure to data subjects.

7. **No data-subject rights handling for filing-related processing**  
   GDPR requires notice of rights of access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, portability where applicable, and complaint to a supervisory authority under **GDPR Art. 13(2)(b)–(d)** and **Art. 14(2)(c)–(e)**.  
   *SOP gap:* No step requires confirming how data-subject rights apply to data used in regulatory filings, including where restrictions may apply due to legal obligations.

8. **No further-processing assessment**  
   Where personal data is processed for a purpose other than that for which it was collected, GDPR requires additional information before further processing under **GDPR Art. 13(3)** and **Art. 14(4)**.  
   *SOP gap:* Regulatory filing use may be secondary to the original collection purpose, but the SOP has no compatibility or further-processing review step.

## Missing international transfer elements

9. **No transfer mechanism selection and documentation requirement**  
   GDPR requires transfers to third countries or international organizations to comply with Chapter V, and the level of protection may not be undermined under **GDPR Art. 44**. Transfers may rely on adequacy decisions under **GDPR Art. 45**, appropriate safeguards under **GDPR Art. 46**, binding corporate rules under **GDPR Art. 47**, or derogations under **GDPR Art. 49**.  
   *SOP gap:* §4.6 says “appropriate safeguards are in place,” but does not require documenting which mechanism applies.

10. **No requirement to disclose transfer safeguards to data subjects**  
   Where transfers occur, GDPR requires notice of the intended transfer and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision, or reference to safeguards and how to obtain a copy under **GDPR Art. 13(1)(f)** and **Art. 14(1)(f)**. Data subjects also have a right to be informed of safeguards under **GDPR Art. 15(2)**.  
   *SOP gap:* No filing workflow step requires confirming that privacy notices disclose EU-to-U.S. transfers to analytics teams or explain the applicable safeguards.

11. **No Transfer Impact Assessment / supplementary-measures process**  
   While Chapter V requires effective protection for transferred personal data under **GDPR Art. 44** and **Art. 46**, the SOP does not require assessment that the chosen safeguards are effective in the destination country.  
   *SOP gap:* There is no required transfer risk assessment, legal review of destination-country access risks, or supplementary technical/organizational measures for EU-to-U.S. regulatory filing support.

12. **No SCC or other safeguard inventory**  
   Appropriate safeguards may include standard data protection clauses, binding corporate rules, approved codes of conduct, certification mechanisms, or other approved instruments under **GDPR Art. 46(2)** and **Art. 47**.  
   *SOP gap:* The SOP does not require evidence that SCCs, BCRs, or another valid mechanism are executed before U.S.-based analytics teams or vendors access EU personal data.

13. **No process for adequacy versus non-adequacy destinations**  
   Transfers may proceed on the basis of an adequacy decision under **GDPR Art. 45**, while non-adequate destinations require safeguards or derogations under **GDPR Art. 46** or **Art. 49**.  
   *SOP gap:* The SOP scope includes the U.S., UK, Canada, Singapore, EU/EEA, but does not require destination-country mapping or adequacy-status checks.

14. **No controls for onward transfers**  
   GDPR’s transfer rules apply to transfers of personal data to third countries or international organizations under **GDPR Art. 44**.  
   *SOP gap:* The SOP does not address whether U.S.-based analytics teams, external counsel, cloud providers, filing portals, or vendors may onward-transfer EU personal data, or what contractual controls apply.

15. **No Article 49 derogation governance**  
   Derogations for specific situations are limited and conditional under **GDPR Art. 49**.  
   *SOP gap:* The SOP does not prohibit routine reliance on derogations, require legal approval for derogation use, or require documentation of the specific derogation and conditions.

16. **No Article 48 control for foreign regulatory demands**  
   A judgment or decision of a third-country court or administrative authority requiring transfer or disclosure of personal data is recognized or enforceable only if based on an international agreement, without prejudice to other transfer grounds, under **GDPR Art. 48**.  
   *SOP gap:* §5.6 covers regulatory inquiries generally, but does not require GDPR-specific legal review before disclosing EU personal data to non-EU regulators.

17. **No ROPA/transfer-record linkage**  
   Records of processing must include, where applicable, transfers of personal data to a third country or international organization and documentation of suitable safeguards for certain transfers under **GDPR Art. 30(1)(e)**.  
   *SOP gap:* §3.1 mentions ROPA summaries when requested by a supervisory authority, but the filing process does not require updating or checking ROPA entries for each regulatory filing involving EU personal data.

## Bottom line

SOP-LEGC-005 has a general GDPR statement, DPO ownership, and filing governance, but it is missing operational controls for: privacy-notice content, Article 14 notices, legal basis/purpose mapping, recipient and retention disclosures, transfer mechanism selection, safeguard documentation, transfer-risk assessment, data-subject disclosure of transfer safeguards, onward-transfer controls, Article 48 review, and ROPA linkage.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing transparency and transfer elements from the reference, and correctly cites GDPR Articles 13, 14, 30, and Chapter V provisions.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-ITOP-005 (Capacity Planning and Management) against SOC 2 Common Criteria CC7 (monitoring) and CC8 (change management), what specific controls are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) scheduled tabletop exercises for incident-response scenarios (CC7.4) — the plan exists but periodic testing is absent; (2) defined alert thresholds and escalation paths for capacity-related anomalies (CC7.2/7.3); (3) explicit segregation of duties between change requester, approver, and implementer for capacity changes (CC8.1); (4) post-incident review procedures feeding lessons learned back to monitoring and change processes (CC7.5).

output.answer:
Below are the **specific missing controls** in SOP-ITOP-005 when assessed against **SOC 2 Common Criteria CC7 — System Operations / Monitoring** and **CC8 — Change Management**.

SOC 2 basis: **CC7.1–CC7.5** cover detection/monitoring of configuration changes, vulnerabilities, anomalies, security events, incident evaluation, response, and recovery. **CC8.1** requires changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures to be authorized, designed/developed/configured, documented, tested, approved, and implemented. Source: AICPA Trust Services Criteria, CC7 and CC8.1; public copy: https://arpio.io/wp-content/uploads/2020/08/trust-services-criteria.pdf.

---

## Missing CC7 Monitoring Controls

| SOC 2 criterion | Missing control | Why it is missing in SOP-ITOP-005 |
|---|---|---|
| **CC7.1 — Detect configuration changes that introduce vulnerabilities and susceptibilities to newly discovered vulnerabilities** | **Formal vulnerability monitoring tied to capacity-related infrastructure changes** | The SOP monitors capacity metrics such as CPU, memory, latency, Kafka lag, storage, and SLO breaches, but it does not require vulnerability scans or security assessment after capacity changes such as new instance types, new nodes, Kafka partition expansion, Snowflake scaling, GPU fleet changes, or load balancer/network changes. SOC 2 CC7.1 expects detection of configuration changes that introduce vulnerabilities and susceptibilities to newly discovered vulnerabilities. |
| **CC7.1** | **Defined secure configuration standards for capacity-managed assets** | The SOP defines performance baselines in Section 5.1, but it does not define secure configuration baselines or approved hardened build standards for EC2, EKS nodes, RDS, Snowflake, Kafka, Redis, Pinecone, SageMaker, or GPU instances. SOC 2 CC7.1 expects monitoring against configuration standards to detect changes that create vulnerabilities. |
| **CC7.1** | **Automated configuration drift detection across IaC-managed resources** | Section 6.1 states that manual AWS console changes are logged in CloudTrail and trigger a Security Hub finding, but the SOP does not require routine automated comparison of production state against Terraform/Helm desired state, nor does it require remediation of drift back into IaC. SOC 2 CC7.1 expects monitoring for configuration changes that may introduce vulnerabilities. |
| **CC7.1 / CC7.2** | **Unauthorized or unknown component detection** | The SOP does not define controls to detect unauthorized infrastructure components, rogue instances, unapproved containers, unapproved Kafka topics, unexpected Snowflake warehouses, or unmanaged cloud resources. SOC 2 CC7.1 and CC7.2 expect monitoring procedures that identify security-relevant changes and anomalous system activity. |
| **CC7.2 — Monitor system components for anomalies indicative of malicious acts, natural disasters, or errors** | **Security event monitoring beyond capacity/performance telemetry** | Section 5.2 focuses on Datadog capacity thresholds, synthetic checks, and Kafka lag. It does not define monitoring for authentication anomalies, privilege escalation, unusual API calls, malware indicators, suspicious network flows, anomalous data access, container runtime events, or cloud control-plane events. SOC 2 CC7.2 expects monitoring of system components for anomalies and security events, not only performance exhaustion. |
| **CC7.2** | **Centralized security logging / SIEM correlation control** | The SOP references Datadog, ServiceNow, PagerDuty, CloudTrail, and Security Hub, but it does not require centralized log aggregation, SIEM correlation, retention, alert tuning, or documented review of security events. SOC 2 CC7.2 expects monitoring procedures capable of identifying anomalies and security events across system components. |
| **CC7.2** | **Monitoring of third-party security and availability events** | Section 4.6 requires vendors to provide capacity and availability metrics, but it does not require monitoring vendor security advisories, provider outage notices, compromise indicators, or third-party incident notifications for AWS, Snowflake, Pinecone, Datadog, GitHub, ServiceNow, or PagerDuty. SOC 2 CC7.2 expects monitoring of relevant system components and operations, including externally operated components when they affect the system. |
| **CC7.3 — Evaluate security events to determine whether they are security incidents** | **Documented security event triage criteria** | The SOP says capacity-related SLO breaches become incidents under Section 4.5 and Section 5.2, but it does not define criteria for evaluating security events, suspicious configuration changes, anomalous access, or vulnerability alerts to determine whether they are incidents. SOC 2 CC7.3 expects evaluation of security events to determine whether they qualify as incidents. |
| **CC7.3** | **Required documentation of security event analysis** | The SOP requires ServiceNow incidents for capacity thresholds and PIRs for P1/P2 capacity incidents, but it does not require documentation of security event triage, impact analysis, affected assets, evidence reviewed, false-positive rationale, or escalation decisions. SOC 2 CC7.3 expects security events to be evaluated and handled through defined procedures. |
| **CC7.4 — Respond to identified security incidents** | **Capacity-related security incident response playbooks** | The SOP mentions DDoS only as an example in Section 3 and references SOP-ISEC-002 in Section 4.5, but it does not include or require specific capacity-related security response playbooks, such as DDoS mitigation, autoscaling abuse, cloud resource hijacking, crypto-mining, bot traffic, credential-driven resource exhaustion, or malicious workload spikes. SOC 2 CC7.4 expects defined response to identified security incidents. |
| **CC7.4** | **Scheduled incident response exercises / tabletop testing** | Section 4.5 explicitly states that regular tabletop exercises to rehearse response procedures are **not currently scheduled**. This is a direct missing control for demonstrating operational readiness to respond to capacity-driven outages or security-related capacity events. SOC 2 CC7.4 expects the entity to respond to identified security incidents using established procedures. |
| **CC7.5 — Recover from identified security incidents** | **Security incident recovery criteria and validation** | Section 5.5 requires PIRs after P1/P2 capacity incidents, but the SOP does not define security recovery steps, restoration validation, integrity checks, security hardening after recovery, or criteria for returning systems to normal operations after security-related capacity incidents. SOC 2 CC7.5 expects recovery activities for identified security incidents. |
| **CC7.5** | **Post-incident remediation tracking for security findings** | Section 5.5 creates Jira action items for capacity gaps, but it does not require tracking of security remediation items such as patching, key rotation, IAM cleanup, WAF rule tuning, detection-rule updates, or vulnerability closure after security-related incidents. SOC 2 CC7.5 expects recovery and remediation activities following security incidents. |

---

## Missing CC8 Change Management Controls

| SOC 2 criterion | Missing control | Why it is missing in SOP-ITOP-005 |
|---|---|---|
| **CC8.1 — Authorize, design/develop/acquire/configure, document, test, approve, and implement changes** | **Pre-production testing requirement for all capacity changes** | Section 5.4 requires `terraform plan` or `helm diff`, but those are change previews, not full testing. The SOP does not require staging/UAT deployment, load testing, regression testing, resilience testing, or security testing before production capacity changes. SOC 2 CC8.1 explicitly includes testing of changes. |
| **CC8.1** | **Rollback plans for all capacity changes** | Section 5.4.2 requires a rollback plan for vertical scaling, but Section 5.4.1 horizontal scaling does not require one. The SOP also does not define rollback plans for Kafka partition expansion, database replica promotion, autoscaling policy changes, Snowflake warehouse scaling, network bandwidth changes, or GPU endpoint changes. SOC 2 CC8.1 requires controlled implementation of changes, which includes documented backout/rollback procedures. |
| **CC8.1** | **Emergency change control with retroactive approval and reconciliation** | Section 8 defines an exception process, and Section 6.1 logs break-glass manual console changes, but the SOP does not require emergency changes to be retroactively documented in ServiceNow, peer-reviewed, risk-assessed, approved after the fact, tested, and reconciled back into Terraform/Helm. SOC 2 CC8.1 requires changes to be authorized, documented, approved, and implemented under change control. |
| **CC8.1** | **Change control for monitoring thresholds, baselines, dashboards, and alert logic** | Section 5.1 defines baselines and Section 5.2 defines thresholds, but the SOP does not require formal change requests, testing, approval, or version history for changes to Datadog monitors, alert thresholds, synthetic checks, dashboard definitions, or baseline formulas. These changes affect monitoring effectiveness and should be controlled under SOC 2 CC8.1. |
| **CC8.1** | **Change control for capacity models and forecasting logic** | Section 5.3 uses Akkadian Pro forecasting models, but the SOP does not require approval, validation, versioning, or change documentation for model assumptions, forecast formulas, demand inputs, or capacity planning logic. Because these models drive infrastructure decisions, changes should be documented and approved under SOC 2 CC8.1. |
| **CC8.1** | **Change control for runbooks and procedures** | The SOP contains runbooks such as `run-scale-out-h-001` and `run-scale-up-v-001`, but it does not define a control requiring updates to capacity runbooks, SOP procedures, or operational scripts to be reviewed, approved, tested, version-controlled, and communicated. SOC 2 CC8.1 applies to changes to procedures as well as infrastructure, data, and software. |
| **CC8.1** | **Explicit ServiceNow change record linkage for horizontal scaling** | Section 4.4 says production infrastructure capacity changes must be authorized via ServiceNow, but the horizontal scaling runbook in Section 5.4.1 only describes Git branch, PR, peer approval, merge, validation, and ServiceNow incident/alert update. It does not explicitly require a ServiceNow Change Request ID, risk classification, implementation window, or approval evidence for horizontal scaling. SOC 2 CC8.1 requires changes to be authorized, documented, approved, and implemented. |
| **CC8.1** | **Post-implementation review for non-incident capacity changes** | Section 5.5 requires PIRs after P1/P2 capacity incidents, but the SOP does not require post-implementation review for planned capacity changes, forecast-driven scaling, maintenance-window changes, or high-risk infrastructure changes that do not originate from incidents. SOC 2 CC8.1 expects changes to be implemented in a controlled manner, including confirming successful implementation. |
| **CC8.1** | **CMDB / asset inventory update requirement after capacity changes** | Section 5.1 references the Capacity Management Database, but scaling procedures do not require updating the CMDB, asset inventory, ownership, tags, dependencies, or service mapping after capacity changes. SOC 2 CC8.1 requires documented and controlled changes to infrastructure and related system components. |
| **CC8.1** | **Security impact assessment for capacity changes** | Section 4.4 requires the Change Request to identify capacity impact, but it does not require a security impact assessment for changes that alter compute, storage, network, database, or AI/ML infrastructure. SOC 2 CC8.1 requires controlled changes, and CC7.1 also expects detection of changes that introduce vulnerabilities. |
| **CC8.1** | **Production data protection controls during scaling changes** | The SOP covers infrastructure scaling but does not address whether capacity changes involving databases, Snowflake, Kafka, RDS replicas, Redis, or Pinecone require data migration validation, backup verification, data integrity checks, or privacy/security review before implementation. SOC 2 CC8.1 applies to changes to infrastructure, data, software, and procedures. |

---

## Key conclusion

SOP-ITOP-005 is **strong for capacity monitoring and basic IaC-based approval**, especially for performance thresholds, alerting, peer review, segregation of duties, ServiceNow integration, and post-incident review for capacity incidents.

However, it is **missing several SOC 2 CC7 controls** because monitoring is mostly limited to capacity/performance telemetry and does not fully cover security-event monitoring, vulnerability/configuration monitoring, unauthorized component detection, event triage, security incident response exercises, or security recovery.

It is also **missing several SOC 2 CC8 controls** because change management is not consistently applied to all relevant change types, especially monitoring thresholds, baselines, forecasting models, runbooks, emergency changes, rollback plans, pre-production testing, CMDB updates, and security impact assessments.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer correctly identifies all four missing controls from the reference (tabletop exercises, alert thresholds/escalation, segregation of duties, post-incident review) and provides extensive additional detail, but its citations reference the full CC7.1–CC7.5 and CC8.1 range without specifically highlighting CC7.2/7.3/7.4/7.5/8.1 as the expected sections.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-LEGC-005 (Regulatory Filing and Reporting) against HIPAA's breach-reporting and de-identification requirements, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) de-identification methodology for PHI appearing in filings — neither Safe Harbor (§164.514(b)(2)) nor Expert Determination (§164.514(b)(1)) is specified; (2) breach-notification procedures with timelines (§164.404 60-day individual notice, §164.408 HHS reporting), required notice content, and the low-probability-of-compromise risk assessment; (3) minimum-necessary policy (§164.502(b)) for PHI accessible to filing personnel; (4) accounting-of-disclosures procedures (§164.528) for filing-related disclosures.
question.notes: Gap detection paired with the sop_compliance question for the same SOP.

output.answer:
SOP-LEGC-005 is **missing substantial HIPAA-specific breach-reporting and de-identification content**. It is a general regulatory-filing SOP and references HIPAA only at a high level in scope, but it does not operationalize the HIPAA Breach Notification Rule or HIPAA de-identification standards.

## Missing HIPAA breach-reporting elements

### 1. HIPAA breach trigger and “unsecured PHI” standard
The SOP does not define when a HIPAA breach notification obligation is triggered.

Missing elements:
- Definition of a “breach” as an impermissible acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of PHI that compromises the security or privacy of the PHI. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.402**
- Linkage to **“unsecured protected health information”** as the core trigger for notification. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.402, 164.404**
- The three HIPAA breach exceptions:
  - Unintentional good-faith access/use by workforce member or person acting under authority;
  - Inadvertent disclosure between authorized persons within the same covered entity/business associate/organized health care arrangement;
  - Good-faith belief that the unauthorized recipient could not reasonably retain the information. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.402**

### 2. Required HIPAA breach risk assessment
The SOP lacks a HIPAA-specific risk-assessment process for determining whether an impermissible PHI use/disclosure is presumed to be a reportable breach or whether there is a low probability that PHI was compromised.

Missing required assessment factors:
- Nature and extent of PHI involved, including types of identifiers and likelihood of re-identification;
- Unauthorized person who used the PHI or to whom disclosure was made;
- Whether the PHI was actually acquired or viewed;
- Extent to which the risk to PHI was mitigated. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.402**

### 3. Notification to affected individuals
The SOP does not specify HIPAA’s individual-notice requirement.

Missing elements:
- Notice to each affected individual whose unsecured PHI has been, or is reasonably believed to have been, breached. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.404(a)**
- Timing: without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.404(b)**
- Permitted delivery methods, including written notice by first-class mail, email if agreed to, substitute notice if contact information is insufficient, and urgent telephone/other notice where imminent misuse is possible. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.404(d)**

### 4. Required content of individual breach notices
The SOP does not define the content required in HIPAA breach notices.

Missing required notice content:
- Brief description of what happened, including breach date and discovery date, if known;
- Description of the types of unsecured PHI involved;
- Steps individuals should take to protect themselves;
- Description of what Meridian is doing to investigate, mitigate harm, and protect against further breaches;
- Contact procedures for questions, including toll-free telephone number, email address, website, or postal address. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.404(c)**

### 5. Media notification for large breaches
The SOP does not include HIPAA’s media-notification requirement.

Missing elements:
- If a breach involves more than 500 residents of a state or jurisdiction, notice must be provided to prominent media outlets serving that state or jurisdiction. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.406(a)**
- Timing: without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.406(b)**
- Content aligned to the individual-notice content requirements. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.406(c)**

### 6. HHS Secretary notification
The SOP does not specify reporting to HHS/OCR.

Missing elements:
- For breaches involving 500 or more individuals: notify the Secretary of HHS contemporaneously with individual notice and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.408(b)**
- For breaches involving fewer than 500 individuals: maintain a log and submit to HHS no later than 60 days after the end of the calendar year. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.408(c)**

### 7. Business associate notification workflow
The SOP defines “BA” but does not include the required business-associate breach-notification workflow.

Missing elements:
- Business associate must notify the covered entity of a breach of unsecured PHI. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.410(a)**
- Timing: without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days after discovery. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.410(b)**
- Required information: identification of each affected individual, if known, and other available information required for the covered entity’s notice. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.410(c)**

### 8. Breach discovery rule
The SOP does not define when a HIPAA breach is deemed “discovered.”

Missing element:
- A breach is treated as discovered on the first day the breach is known to the covered entity/business associate, or would have been known by exercising reasonable diligence. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.404(a)(2), 164.410(a)(2)**

### 9. Law-enforcement delay procedure
The SOP does not include the HIPAA law-enforcement delay mechanism.

Missing elements:
- Procedure to delay notification if a law-enforcement official states that notification would impede a criminal investigation or damage national security. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.412**
- Distinction between written statement-based delay and oral statement-based delay. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.412**

### 10. HIPAA-specific documentation, policies, sanctions, and complaint handling
The SOP has archival controls, but it does not incorporate HIPAA’s administrative requirements for breach-notification compliance.

Missing elements:
- Policies and procedures for HIPAA breach notification. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.414**
- Workforce training. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.414, 164.530(b)**
- Complaint process. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.414, 164.530(d)**
- Sanctions for workforce noncompliance. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.414, 164.530(e)**
- Documentation retention for required actions, activities, and designations. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. §§ 164.414, 164.530(j)**

---

## Missing HIPAA de-identification elements

### 11. No HIPAA de-identification standard
The SOP does not describe when PHI is considered de-identified.

Missing element:
- Health information is not individually identifiable if it does not identify an individual and there is no reasonable basis to believe it can be used to identify an individual. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(a)**

### 12. No Expert Determination method
The SOP does not include the HIPAA Expert Determination pathway.

Missing elements:
- A qualified person with appropriate knowledge of and experience with generally accepted statistical and scientific principles must determine that the risk is very small that the information could identify an individual. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(1)**
- The expert must document the methods and results of the analysis. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(1)**

### 13. No Safe Harbor method or 18-identifier removal list
The SOP does not include the HIPAA Safe Harbor pathway or require removal of the 18 categories of identifiers.

Missing identifiers include:
- Names;
- Geographic subdivisions smaller than a state, subject to the ZIP-code exception;
- All elements of dates except year, and ages over 89 except aggregation into age 90 or older;
- Telephone numbers;
- Fax numbers;
- Email addresses;
- Social Security numbers;
- Medical record numbers;
- Health plan beneficiary numbers;
- Account numbers;
- Certificate/license numbers;
- Vehicle identifiers and license plate numbers;
- Device identifiers and serial numbers;
- URLs;
- IP addresses;
- Biometric identifiers;
- Full-face photographs and comparable images;
- Any other unique identifying number, characteristic, or code. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(2)(i)**

The SOP also does not state the Safe Harbor condition that the covered entity must not have actual knowledge that the remaining information could identify the individual. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(2)(ii)**

### 14. No re-identification-code controls
The SOP does not address HIPAA’s rules for codes or keys that permit re-identification.

Missing elements:
- A code or other means of record identification may be assigned only if it is not derived from or related to information about the individual. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(c)**
- The code-reidentification mechanism must not be disclosed. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(c)**
- The code must not be used or disclosed for any other purpose. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(c)**

### 15. No requirement to assess re-identification risk in filings
Because this SOP governs regulatory filings and reporting, it should require evaluation of whether PHI included in filings is identifiable, de-identified, or subject to another HIPAA-permitted disclosure basis. The SOP currently references “data quality” and “evidence packages,” but it does not require:
- PHI minimization before inclusion in regulatory submissions;
- Documentation of whether data are PHI, de-identified data, or limited/other permitted data;
- Expert Determination or Safe Harbor certification before treating filing data as de-identified. **HIPAA: 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(a)-(c)**

---

## Bottom line

SOP-LEGC-005 is strong as a generic filing-governance SOP, but for HIPAA it is missing the operative requirements for:

1. breach identification and risk assessment;  
2. individual, media, HHS, and business-associate notices;  
3. HIPAA-specific timing and notice-content rules;  
4. law-enforcement delay handling;  
5. breach documentation/training/sanctions requirements; and  
6. de-identification methods, identifiers, expert documentation, Safe Harbor conditions, and re-identification-code controls.

Compliance level: gap

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing HIPAA breach-reporting and de-identification elements from the reference, and provides precise regulatory citations for each, including all expected sections.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-AIML-006 (Model Performance Monitoring) against SR 11-7's ongoing monitoring expectations, what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) defined performance thresholds and back-testing cadence tied to model risk tier — monitoring is referenced but specific KPIs and review frequencies are not pinned; (2) challenger-model requirement for high-impact decisions — SR 11-7 expects benchmark/challenger comparisons; (3) escalation path from monitoring findings to senior management and the board (Section V governance); (4) overarching model-risk framework + model inventory anchoring the monitoring activities; (5) explicit linkage between drift signals and re-validation triggers.

expected_citations.section: Section III (Validation), Section IV (Ongoing Monitoring), Section V (Governance)

question.notes: Gap detection paired with the sop_compliance question for same SOP.

output.answer:
Below are the **specific SR 11-7 ongoing-monitoring elements missing or materially incomplete** in **SOP-AIML-006: Model Performance Monitoring**.

| SR 11-7 expectation | What SOP-AIML-006 already has | Missing / gap |
|---|---|---|
| **Process verification** — ongoing monitoring should verify that the model is implemented and operating as intended, including data inputs, processing, implementation logic, and reports. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Ongoing Monitoring,” Process Verification**; Federal Reserve SR 11-7 page: https://www.federalreserve.gov/frrs/guidance/supervisory-guidance-on-model-risk-management.htm | SOP §5.1 requires dashboard onboarding, baseline metrics, data profiles, and monitoring dashboards. SOP §6.2 validates ground-truth metric calculations against Arize for high-risk classification models. | The SOP does **not require a periodic production implementation verification** covering production code, feature transformations, score calculation logic, downstream reports, decision rules, or business-system consumption of model outputs. It focuses on metric monitoring, not full process verification. |
| **Benchmarking** — ongoing monitoring should include benchmarking against alternative models, vendor models, simple heuristics, challenger models, or other reference approaches. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Ongoing Monitoring,” Benchmarking** | SOP §6.1 uses shadow traffic during a 60-minute bake period for new deployments. SOP §2 defines Champion-Challenger. | A formal **benchmarking program is missing**. SOP §2 explicitly states that “a formal Challenger Model governance policy for high-impact decisions is not currently in scope.” There is no requirement to compare production model performance to challenger models, simpler benchmarks, external benchmarks, or alternative methodologies on an ongoing basis. |
| **Outcomes analysis / back-testing** — validation should compare model outputs to actual outcomes and assess whether errors are within acceptable ranges; consistently poor outcomes should trigger redevelopment or corrective action. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Outcomes Analysis,” including Back-Testing** | SOP §5.2 requires performance metrics where ground truth is available. SOP §5.2.2 addresses delayed labels. SOP §6.2 has daily ground-truth validation pipelines for high-risk classification models. | The SOP lacks a **formal back-testing / outcomes-analysis procedure** across all relevant model classes. It does not define back-testing frequency, sample selection, outcome windows, statistical tests, acceptable error distributions, or documentation standards. The alert matrix in SOP §5.4 is mainly AUROC/F1/drift oriented and does not establish outcome-error thresholds for regression, ranking, summarization, calibration, or financial-loss impact. |
| **Override analysis** — ongoing monitoring should include analysis and documentation of cases where users ignore, alter, or reverse model outputs; high override rates may indicate model weakness. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Ongoing Monitoring,” Overrides** | SOP §5.5 includes incident response when model performance alerts fire. | The SOP does **not require tracking, documenting, trending, or reviewing human overrides** of model outputs. This is a clear SR 11-7 gap, especially for clinical decision support, financial decisioning, triage, and support-copilot use cases where users may override recommendations. |
| **Monitoring of model assumptions and limitations** — ongoing monitoring should assess whether model assumptions, limitations, and use conditions remain valid as products, markets, customers, or business practices change. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Ongoing Monitoring”; §III “Model Development, Implementation, and Use” regarding model limitations and assumptions** | SOP §5.3 monitors data drift and prediction drift. SOP §8.2 escalates unresolved degradation after 7 days. | The SOP does **not require periodic review of model assumptions, limitations, intended-use boundaries, or environmental changes**. Drift monitoring alone does not satisfy SR 11-7’s expectation to assess whether the model remains appropriate under changed business, clinical, population, financial, regulatory, or operating conditions. |
| **Verification that the model is used as intended** — model risk management should ensure models are used for approved purposes and users understand limitations. **SR 11-7, §III “Model Development, Implementation, and Use”; §IV “Model Validation – Ongoing Monitoring”** | SOP §1.2 lists model categories and deployment environments. SOP §5.1 requires registry entries with business purpose. | The SOP does **not include monitoring for inappropriate or expanded use**, such as use in an unapproved product, population, geography, clinical workflow, financial decision process, or automated-decision context. It also does not require periodic confirmation that users are applying outputs consistently with approved limitations. |
| **Independent review / validation involvement** — effective model validation should be sufficiently independent from model development and use. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation”; §V “Governance, Policies, and Controls”** | SOP assigns most responsibilities to Model Owners, MLOps, VP Engineering, Director AI/ML, and product stakeholders. | There is **no independent model risk management or validation function** assigned to review ongoing monitoring results, approve monitoring thresholds, assess benchmark results, review back-testing, or challenge remediation decisions. The SOP is operationally strong but not independently governed as SR 11-7 expects. |
| **Risk-based monitoring frequency and validation cadence** — the scope and frequency of ongoing monitoring should be commensurate with model risk and complexity. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Ongoing Monitoring”; §V “Governance, Policies, and Controls”** | SOP §5.2 uses 1-hour, 24-hour, and 7-day metric windows. SOP §7.2 has weekly, monthly, and quarterly reporting. | The SOP does **not define a risk-tiered monitoring and review cadence** tied to model materiality, complexity, business impact, or regulatory significance. Clinical, financial, generative AI, and operational models are all included, but the SOP does not specify SR 11-7-style risk-based validation depth or review frequency. |
| **Documented rationale for thresholds and acceptable error levels** — outcomes analysis should evaluate whether errors remain within acceptable levels; thresholds should be justified and tied to model risk. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation – Outcomes Analysis”; §V “Governance, Policies, and Controls”** | SOP §5.4 defines alert thresholds. SOP §8.1 has exception handling for temporary threshold changes. | The SOP does **not require documented rationale, statistical support, risk appetite linkage, or independent approval** for alert thresholds such as AUROC drop >0.05, PSI ≥0.25, or F1 drop >0.07. Thresholds exist, but SR 11-7 expects acceptability to be justified in relation to model risk and outcome error. |
| **Ongoing monitoring documentation sufficient for validation evidence** — model validation should be documented so findings, limitations, and remediation are traceable. **SR 11-7, §IV “Model Validation”; §V “Governance, Policies, and Controls”** | SOP §5.5 requires incident post-mortems. SOP §7.2 requires periodic reports. | The SOP does **not specify a validation evidence package** for ongoing monitoring, such as periodic validation memos, benchmark results, override reviews, back-testing results, assumption reviews, limitation updates, and independent sign-off. Current documentation is mainly operational reporting and incident documentation. |

**Bottom line:** SOP-AIML-006 has strong operational monitoring for drift, performance metrics, alerting, incident response, and reporting. However, against **SR 11-7’s ongoing monitoring expectations**, it is missing the model-risk-management elements of **process verification, benchmarking, formal outcomes analysis/back-testing, override analysis, assumption/limitation review, intended-use monitoring, independent validation involvement, risk-based review cadence, and documented threshold rationale**.

Compliance level: partial

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer comprehensively identifies all missing SR 11-7 elements with precise section references, matching the reference's expectations and citations.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-CLIN-005 (Diagnostic AI Accuracy and Calibration) against NIST AI RMF's MEASURE function (including the GenAI Profile where relevant), what specific elements are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) bias/fairness/equity testing methodology beyond accuracy — demographic-stratified performance metrics, calibration across protected groups (MEASURE 2/4); (2) AI risk-management framework anchoring measurement to risk tolerance (GOVERN); (3) AI system inventory linking the diagnostic AI to organisational risk (MAP 1); (4) MEASURE coverage of the seven trustworthiness characteristics — safety, security, accountability/transparency, explainability/interpretability, privacy, fair/bias — not just accuracy; (5) post-deployment monitoring closing the loop into MANAGE.
question.notes: Gap detection — clinical AI broader than accuracy.

output.answer: Sorry, need more steps to process this request.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer provides no substantive response to the question and contains no regulatory analysis or citations.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-AIML-009 (Algorithmic Bias Detection and Mitigation) against GDPR Article 35 (DPIA) and Article 22 (automated decision-making), what specific procedures are MISSING?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) procedure under Article 35 — systematic description of processing, necessity/proportionality assessment, risks-to-rights evaluation, measures envisaged; (2) prior-consultation procedure with the supervisory authority (Article 36) when residual risk remains high; (3) Article 22 procedures for solely-automated significant-effect decisions — meaningful information about the logic involved, right to obtain human intervention, right to express the data subject's point of view, right to contest the decision; (4) Articles 13/14 specificity in privacy notices regarding the bias-detection processing and its purposes.
question.notes: Gap detection across multiple GDPR articles for a bias SOP.

scores.judge_rationale: The candidate answer correctly identifies all missing procedures from the reference (DPIA, Article 22 safeguards, Articles 13/14 notices) and provides detailed sub-procedures, but it cites GDPR articles generally without always specifying the exact paragraph for each point, making citations partially complete.

question.question: Reviewing SOP-PENG-001 (Secure Software Development Lifecycle) against EU AI Act Article 9 (risk management system), what specific elements are MISSING for an SDLC supporting high-risk AI systems?
question.expected_answer: Missing: (1) continuous lifecycle risk-management process (Article 9 §2) — identification/analysis/estimation/evaluation of risks throughout development and after deployment, integrated into the SDLC phases; (2) elimination or reduction of identified risks through design and development decisions (Article 9 §4); (3) testing procedures explicitly verifying high-risk AI requirements against acceptable residual risk thresholds (Article 9 §6, §7); (4) consideration of foreseeable misuse and the use environment in risk assessment (Article 9 §3); (5) linkage to post-market-monitoring data feeding back into the SDLC (Article 9 §2 lifecycle loop).
question.notes: Gap detection — SDLC needs Article 9 lifecycle risk management baked in.

output.answer:
SOP-PENG-001 is **only partially aligned** with EU AI Act Article 9. It has strong security-SDLC controls, threat modeling, vulnerability scanning, change gates, and post-deployment security monitoring, but it does **not** define a full **AI risk management system** for high-risk AI systems.

Below are the specific missing elements.

| Missing element for high-risk AI SDLC | EU AI Act requirement | What SOP-PENG-001 currently has | Gap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Formal AI risk management system for high-risk AI systems** | Providers of high-risk AI systems must establish, implement, document, and maintain a **risk management system**. **EU AI Act Art. 9(1).** | SOP-PENG-001 defines a Secure SDLC and includes ML artifacts in scope, but its risk process is primarily cybersecurity/privacy-oriented. See SOP §§1.1, 1.2, 5.1–5.6. | Missing an explicit Article 9 AI risk management process applicable to high-risk AI systems, with required AI-specific artifacts, owners, review cadence, and approval gates. |
| **2. Continuous, iterative AI risk process across the full lifecycle** | The risk management system must be a **continuous iterative process**, planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle, with regular systematic review and updates. **EU AI Act Art. 9(2).** | SOP has lifecycle phases and post-deployment vulnerability scanning, but the recurring reviews are mostly security metrics, annual threat models, and vulnerability SLAs. See SOP §§5.6, 7.2. | Missing lifecycle AI-risk review triggers such as model drift, data drift, performance degradation, bias findings, new use contexts, user complaints, adverse events, and post-market monitoring outputs. |
| **3. Identification and analysis of risks to health, safety, and fundamental rights** | The system must identify and analyze known and reasonably foreseeable risks to **health, safety, and fundamental rights** when used as intended. **EU AI Act Art. 9(2)(a).** | Threat modeling uses STRIDE and focuses on spoofing, tampering, information disclosure, DoS, etc. See SOP §5.2.2. | Missing AI harm analysis for clinical, financial, discrimination, accessibility, autonomy, explainability, due process, and fundamental-rights impacts. STRIDE threat modeling is not sufficient for Article 9. |
| **4. Risk assessment for intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse** | The provider must estimate and evaluate risks arising from intended use and **reasonably foreseeable misuse**. **EU AI Act Art. 9(2)(b).** | SOP includes “abuser stories” for security misuse, e.g., brute force, IDOR, injection, XSS. See SOP §§5.2.2, 5.4.1. | Missing AI-specific foreseeable misuse scenarios, such as overreliance by clinicians, use outside intended patient population, unsupported clinical decision use, automation bias, adversarial prompting, data leakage through model outputs, or use by untrained deployers. |
| **5. Integration with post-market monitoring data** | The risk system must evaluate other risks based on analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system. **EU AI Act Art. 9(2)(c); Art. 72.** | SOP has post-deployment security/vulnerability monitoring using Wiz, Snyk, Datadog, Splunk, and ServiceNow. See SOP §§5.6, 7.1. | Missing Article 72-style post-market AI monitoring inputs: real-world performance, complaints, serious incidents, near misses, bias reports, human oversight failures, model drift, and deployer feedback. |
| **6. AI-specific risk control measures using Article 9 hierarchy** | Risk measures must eliminate or reduce risks as far as technically feasible through design/development; where appropriate, implement mitigation/control measures; and provide information/training to deployers. **EU AI Act Art. 9(5)(a)–(c).** | SOP requires security mitigations, compensating controls, risk acceptance, and secure design. See SOP §§5.2.2, 5.2.3, 8. | Missing an AI risk-control hierarchy covering model design changes, dataset constraints, output limitations, confidence thresholds, human-in-the-loop controls, intended-use restrictions, escalation procedures, and deployer instructions/training. |
| **7. Residual risk acceptability for each AI hazard and overall residual risk** | Risk management measures must ensure the relevant residual risk associated with each hazard and the overall residual risk are judged acceptable. **EU AI Act Art. 9(4).** | SOP threat models include inherent and residual security risk using Meridian’s risk matrix. See SOP §5.2.2. | Missing Article 9 acceptance criteria for AI hazards, including who can accept residual AI risk, what “acceptable” means for high-risk AI, and how health/safety/fundamental-rights risks are evaluated separately from cybersecurity risk. |
| **8. Consideration of interactions among EU AI Act requirements** | Risk management measures must consider effects and interactions from applying the high-risk AI requirements in Section 2, including data governance, documentation, logging, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity. **EU AI Act Art. 9(3), read with Arts. 10–15.** | SOP addresses cybersecurity, logging, encryption, SAST/SCA, artifact signing, and some ML pipeline security. See SOP §§4.1, 4.2, 5.3, 5.4. | Missing a control framework mapping AI risks to Articles 10–15: training/validation/test data governance, technical documentation, AI event logging, transparency/instructions for use, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, and model-specific cybersecurity. |
| **9. AI performance and safety testing against predefined metrics and thresholds** | Testing procedures must identify appropriate risk management measures and ensure the high-risk AI system performs consistently for its intended purpose and complies with Chapter III, Section 2 requirements. Testing must be done before placing on the market or putting into service and before substantial modification. **EU AI Act Art. 9(6)–(7).** | SOP has SAST, SCA, IaC scanning, DAST, penetration testing, and security QA. See SOP §§5.3.3, 5.4. | Missing AI validation testing: accuracy, robustness, bias/fairness, calibration, explainability, clinical/operational safety, human oversight effectiveness, subgroup performance, stress testing, adversarial testing, and acceptance thresholds before release/substantial modification. |
| **10. Defined intended purpose and AI use-boundaries in requirements** | Article 9 risk assessment is anchored to risks when the system is used in accordance with its **intended purpose** and foreseeable misuse. **EU AI Act Art. 9(2)(a)–(b).** | SOP requirements capture business, functional, security, privacy, and regulatory considerations. See SOP §5.1. | Missing mandatory intended-purpose documentation for each high-risk AI system, including target users, affected persons, operating environment, contraindicated uses, limitations, and prohibited/off-label uses. |
| **11. Vulnerable groups and minors analysis** | Providers must consider whether the high-risk AI system is likely to adversely affect persons under 18 or other vulnerable groups. **EU AI Act Art. 9(9).** | SOP flags ePHI/PII and privacy, but does not require vulnerability-group analysis. See SOP §5.1. | Missing assessment of impacts on children, elderly patients, disabled persons, economically vulnerable persons, non-native speakers, minority populations, or other vulnerable groups relevant to Meridian’s clinical/financial products. |
| **12. Human oversight risk controls** | High-risk AI systems must be designed to allow effective human oversight, and Article 9 risk controls must consider interaction with Section 2 requirements. **EU AI Act Art. 9(3); Art. 14.** | SOP does not define human oversight requirements for ML outputs; it focuses on security approvals and engineering review. See SOP §§3, 5.2–5.5. | Missing human oversight design requirements: when humans must review AI outputs, override mechanisms, alerting, escalation, prevention of automation bias, competency/training requirements, and auditability of human decisions. |
| **13. Data governance risk controls** | Article 9 risk measures must interact with Section 2 requirements, including data governance for training, validation, and testing data. **EU AI Act Art. 9(3); Art. 10.** | SOP references data classification, DFDs, privacy, and secure ML pipelines. See SOP §§1.2, 5.1, 5.2. | Missing AI dataset controls: representativeness, relevance, accuracy, completeness, bias assessment, data provenance, labeling quality, data splits, population coverage, and suitability for intended purpose. |
| **14. AI system logging/recordkeeping beyond security logs** | Article 9 risk controls must account for record-keeping/logging obligations for high-risk AI systems. **EU AI Act Art. 9(3); Art. 12.** | SOP defines audit logs for ePHI access and security events. See SOP §4.2, especially SSDLC-HIPAA-002. | Missing AI operational logs: model version, input/output metadata, confidence scores, human override events, performance exceptions, monitoring alerts, drift indicators, and traceability needed to investigate AI-specific incidents. |
| **15. Transparency and deployer instructions** | Risk controls include providing required information under Article 13 and, where appropriate, training to deployers. **EU AI Act Art. 9(5)(c); Art. 13.** | SOP has internal engineering training and security documentation, but not deployer-facing AI instructions. See SOP §9. | Missing deployer/user instructions: intended purpose, limitations, expected accuracy, input data requirements, human oversight instructions, monitoring obligations, known risks, residual risks, and misuse warnings. |
| **16. Substantial modification trigger for AI re-assessment** | Testing must occur before placing on the market/putting into service and before substantial modification. **EU AI Act Art. 9(7).** | SOP requires gates for software changes and significant enhancements, but does not define AI “substantial modification.” See SOP §§5.1–5.5. | Missing criteria for when model retraining, dataset changes, threshold changes, new deployment context, new user population, or model architecture changes trigger Article 9 re-risk assessment and re-testing. |
| **17. AI-specific post-deployment incident/adverse-impact escalation** | Article 9 requires ongoing risk review using lifecycle and post-market data. **EU AI Act Art. 9(2), 9(2)(c); Art. 72.** | SOP routes security incidents to SOP-ITOP-001 and vulnerabilities to SOP-ITOP-005. See SOP §§5.6, 10.1. | Missing AI adverse-event escalation: discriminatory outputs, unsafe recommendations, high false negatives/positives, human oversight failures, complaints from deployers/affected persons, and serious incident triage. |

**Bottom line:** SOP-PENG-001 is a mature **secure software** SDLC, but for **high-risk AI systems** it needs a dedicated Article 9 overlay or integrated AI-risk procedure covering intended purpose, fundamental-rights/health/safety risk analysis, foreseeable misuse, AI testing, residual AI risk acceptance, vulnerable groups, human oversight, data governance, transparency, and post-market AI monitoring.

Compliance level: partial
